claim: The most valuable part of AI evals is manually analyzing production traces and taking notes—this 'data accounting' step delivers 80% of evaluation value
actionable: Before building any automated eval, spend 1-2 hours manually reading 100 real production traces and writing problem observations; this often eliminates need for sophisticated metrics
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Binary pass/fail eval scores are dramatically superior to 1-5 Likert scales; average score 3.2 vs 3.7 is meaningless and impossible to act on
actionable: Replace all continuous rating scales with binary pass/fail in your evals; if you must use scales, require rubrics and strict calibration; default to binary for 95% of evals
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Agreement metrics between human and LLM judge are a trap and often mask poor eval quality; 90% agreement can be achieved by always predicting negative
actionable: Never report 'agreement' as an eval metric; instead measure true positive rate and true negative rate separately; plot a confusion matrix to reveal where your judge is wrong
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: The axial coding technique (grouping open-coded observations into categories) quickly reveals the dominant failure modes in your system
actionable: After taking raw notes on 100 traces, use an LLM to categorize them into 5-6 failure buckets; create a pivot table to count occurrences and identify your top problems
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Use dimensions (persona, customer type, scenario) to generate synthetic test cases, not raw LLM requests; this ensures you explore the input space comprehensively
actionable: Define 3-4 key dimensions for your problem space; ask an LLM to generate test cases for each dimension combination; this beats random synthetic data generation
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Code-based evals (assertions, deterministic checks) are cheaper than LLM-as-judge evals and should be used for any problem with a ground-truth answer
actionable: For each failure mode, ask: 'Is there a deterministic check I can do?' If yes, write a code-based eval first; only use LLM judge for subjective or complex cases
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: When building LLM-as-judge evals, hold out test data; if you include all examples in your prompt, the judge will overfit and score 100%
actionable: Split your labeled data 80/20; use 80% for few-shot examples in your judge prompt; test judge on held-out 20%; iterate on failures without looking at test set
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Look at minimum 100 real traces before deciding what to optimize; once you reach 'theoretical saturation' (learning nothing new), you can stop
actionable: Set 100 traces as minimum goal; people typically get addicted after 20-30 and continue naturally; this is the point at which patterns become obvious
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Generic 'helpfulness' and 'toxicity' evals from vendors rarely match your actual product problems; they lead teams astray by consuming energy on irrelevant metrics
actionable: Do not use off-the-shelf eval frameworks as primary metrics; use them only as sampling mechanisms to find interesting traces; build product-specific evals from trace analysis
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Keep your eval suite small (under a dozen evals in most cases); maintaining and interpreting many judges is expensive and creates confusion
actionable: For each new problem, ask yourself: 'Is this in my top 3 failure modes?' Only build an eval if the answer is yes; retire evals that stop being relevant
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: In a trace, document both user-facing failures and internal errors that don't surface to users; prioritize fixes based on user impact, not presence of error
actionable: When analyzing traces, distinguish between 'user experienced this problem' vs 'system had this issue but recovered'; weight user-facing failures more heavily in prioritization
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes

claim: Revisit human trace labeling on a cadence (weekly/monthly) and always after major system changes to keep judge calibrated
actionable: Schedule recurring trace review sessions; treat trace analysis like regression testing; whenever you make significant prompt/logic changes, re-label 50+ traces to recalibrate judges
source_title: AI Evaluations Clearly Explained in 50 Minutes
